stem: You are the general practice registrar reviewing a 32-year-old non-Aboriginal woman at her 6 week postnatal visit. She is exclusively breastfeeding her healthy term baby on demand and her menses have not returned. She wants reliable contraception that does not affect her milk supply and is willing to consider any method. She has no history of venous thromboembolism, migraine with aura or breast cancer. Her blood pressure today is 116/72.

data_table.Examination: Well-healed lower segment caesarean scar, uterus involuted, no breast lumps
data_table.Investigations: Cervical screening up to date and normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line contraceptive recommendation?

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill containing 30 micrograms ethinyloestradiol commenced today with seven days of additional condom use to provide ongoing cover
options.rationale: Combined hormonal contraception is avoided in the first 6 weeks postpartum because of venous thromboembolism risk and may reduce milk supply in some breastfeeding women. (Source: Family Planning Australia Postpartum Contraception)

options.text: Depot medroxyprogesterone acetate 150 mg intramuscular injection now, with repeat injection every 12 weeks and no requirement for additional barrier contraception
options.rationale: Depot is effective and breastfeeding-compatible but is not preferred first line because of bone density and weight implications; long-acting reversible contraception is generally preferred when available. (Source: Family Planning Australia Postpartum Contraception)

options.text: Levonorgestrel intrauterine device inserted at this visit with no requirement for additional contraception and a string check in six weeks at her general practitioner
options.rationale: Long-acting reversible contraception with the levonorgestrel IUD is first-line postpartum because it is highly effective, breastfeeding-compatible and avoids combined hormone risks; insertion at 6 weeks postpartum is safe. (Source: Family Planning Australia Postpartum Contraception)

options.text: Lactational amenorrhoea method only, with advice to switch to another contraceptive if menses return or feeding pattern changes from full exclusive breastfeeding
options.rationale: Lactational amenorrhoea requires exclusive breastfeeding, amenorrhoea and infant under 6 months; reliance on it alone has higher failure than long-acting reversible contraception and many women want greater certainty. (Source: Family Planning Australia Postpartum Contraception)

options.text: Progestogen-only pill desogestrel 75 micrograms once daily started today with a 3 hour late-pill window applied if a dose is missed in error at home
options.rationale: Progestogen-only pills are breastfeeding-compatible but daily adherence makes them less effective than long-acting reversible contraception, which is preferred when available. (Source: Family Planning Australia Postpartum Contraception)

explanation.summary: Australian guidance prioritises long-acting reversible contraception postpartum because of high typical-use effectiveness and adherence-free protection. Levonorgestrel and copper intrauterine devices and the etonogestrel implant are all breastfeeding-compatible and can be inserted at or after 6 weeks postpartum (some are inserted immediately after delivery). Combined hormonal methods are avoided until 6 weeks postpartum because of venous thromboembolism risk and possible impact on milk supply. Progestogen-only methods and depot are acceptable alternatives if a LARC is declined.
key_points: Long-acting reversible contraception is first line postpartum
Combined hormonal contraception avoided in first 6 weeks postpartum
Levonorgestrel IUD and etonogestrel implant are breastfeeding compatible
Lactational amenorrhoea requires exclusive breastfeeding, amenorrhoea and infant under 6 months
Depot is effective but second line because of bone density and weight concerns
explanation.pearls: Lactational amenorrhoea provides over 98% protection only while all three conditions hold: exclusive breastfeeding, amenorrhoea and infant under 6 months.

stem: You are the general practice registrar reviewing a 24-year-old non-Aboriginal nulliparous woman with 18 months of progressively severe dysmenorrhoea, deep dyspareunia and cyclical pelvic pain. She is sexually active in a monogamous relationship using condoms. Her cervical screening is up to date and recent pelvic ultrasound shows no endometrioma or adenomyosis. She has no contraindication to combined hormonal contraception. She is not planning pregnancy in the next two years.

data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen, bimanual exam reveals mild tenderness in posterior fornix without nodularity or fixed retroversion
data_table.Investigations: Pelvic ultrasound: no endometrioma, no hydrosalpinx, normal endometrium; FBE and ferritin normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Refer urgently for diagnostic laparoscopy with the option of operative excision of any endometriotic lesions identified at the same procedure
options.rationale: Laparoscopy is reserved for failure of medical therapy, fertility concerns or suspicion of deep infiltrating disease; an empirical hormonal trial is recommended first. (Source: RANZCOG Endometriosis Statement)

options.text: Commence regular paracetamol and oral mefenamic acid during menses only and review symptoms in three months before considering any hormonal therapy
options.rationale: NSAIDs help dysmenorrhoea but do not suppress endometriotic lesions; hormonal suppression is the appropriate next step in suspected endometriosis. (Source: RANZCOG Endometriosis Statement)

options.text: Order serum CA-125 today and refer to gynaecology if the result is elevated above the laboratory cut-off for further evaluation including pelvic MRI
options.rationale: CA-125 is non-specific and elevated in many gynaecological conditions; it is not used to diagnose or monitor endometriosis in routine practice. (Source: RANZCOG Endometriosis Statement)

options.text: Trial continuous combined oral contraceptive pill for three to six months with regular review and refer to gynaecology if symptoms persist despite suppression
options.rationale: Continuous combined hormonal contraception is first-line empiric therapy for suspected endometriosis without contraindication; persistence beyond a 3 to 6 month trial warrants specialist referral. (Source: RANZCOG Endometriosis Statement)

options.text: Commence intramuscular goserelin GnRH agonist therapy with hormonal add-back oestrogen-progestin therapy and review in three months for symptom response
options.rationale: GnRH analogues are reserved for specialist use after failure of first-line hormonal therapy because of cost, bone density effects and the need for add-back. (Source: RANZCOG Endometriosis Statement)

explanation.summary: Australian guidance supports empirical medical therapy for suspected endometriosis with typical history and normal pelvic ultrasound rather than proceeding directly to laparoscopy. Continuous combined hormonal contraception is first line, with progestogen-only options (norethisterone or levonorgestrel IUD) as alternatives. Laparoscopy with excision is reserved for failure of medical therapy after 3 to 6 months, fertility concerns or suspected deep infiltrating disease. CA-125 has no role in routine endometriosis diagnosis or monitoring.
key_points: Continuous COCP is first-line empirical therapy in suspected endometriosis
Laparoscopy reserved for medical failure, fertility planning or deep disease suspicion
CA-125 is non-specific and not used routinely
Levonorgestrel IUD is an effective progestogen-only alternative
GnRH agonists are specialist-initiated with add-back therapy
explanation.pearls: Continuous COCP without a withdrawal bleed (skipping the inactive pills) is the preferred empirical regimen for suspected endometriosis because cyclical menses drive pain.

stem: You are the antenatal clinic registrar reviewing a 27-year-old G2P1 non-Aboriginal woman at 28 weeks gestation. She is rhesus D negative and her antibody screen is negative. Her partner is rhesus D positive. Her previous baby was rhesus D positive and she received postpartum anti-D in 2024. She is currently well with no antepartum bleeding. Her booking ultrasound and current measurements are appropriate for gestation.

data_table.Examination: Fundal height 27 cm, fetal heart heard, no uterine activity
data_table.Investigations: Blood group: O negative, indirect Coombs negative, antibody screen negative; antenatal bloods unremarkable

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Withhold antenatal anti-D because postpartum anti-D was given for the previous pregnancy and repeat the antibody screen at delivery only
options.rationale: Routine antenatal prophylaxis is given to all rhesus D negative women with a negative antibody screen regardless of previous pregnancy or prior anti-D, because antibodies wane and silent sensitisation can occur. (Source: NBA Anti-D Guidelines)

options.text: Order a non-invasive prenatal test for fetal RHD status and defer prophylaxis until the result is available before issuing any anti-D immunoglobulin
options.rationale: Fetal RHD testing is not yet routinely funded in Australia for this purpose; current standard is universal antenatal prophylaxis at 28 and 34 weeks for non-immunised women. (Source: NBA Anti-D Guidelines)

options.text: Administer 625 IU intramuscular anti-D immunoglobulin today and repeat at 34 weeks gestation, then administer postpartum anti-D if the baby is rhesus D positive
options.rationale: Australian routine antenatal prophylaxis uses 625 IU anti-D at 28 weeks and 34 weeks with a postpartum dose if the baby is rhesus D positive; this regimen near eliminates new sensitisations. (Source: NBA Anti-D Guidelines)

options.text: Give a single 1500 IU intramuscular dose of anti-D immunoglobulin at 28 weeks only, omitting the 34 week dose given that her previous baby was unaffected
options.rationale: Australia uses the two-dose 625 IU regimen at 28 and 34 weeks; a single 1500 IU dose is an alternative used elsewhere but is not the current Australian schedule. (Source: NBA Anti-D Guidelines)

options.text: Defer prophylaxis until 36 weeks gestation and combine the routine antenatal dose with cord blood typing arrangements for delivery in the same appointment
options.rationale: Anti-D timing matters because most silent transplacental haemorrhages occur in the third trimester; deferring to 36 weeks leaves a window of unprotected sensitisation. (Source: NBA Anti-D Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Routine antenatal anti-D prophylaxis prevents rhesus D alloimmunisation by neutralising fetal red cells crossing the placenta. The Australian schedule administers 625 IU intramuscular anti-D at 28 and 34 weeks for non-immunised rhesus D negative women, plus postpartum prophylaxis if the baby is rhesus D positive, and an additional dose after any sensitising event such as antepartum bleeding, external cephalic version, abdominal trauma or invasive procedure. Implementation has reduced sensitisation rates below 1% per at-risk pregnancy.
key_points: Routine antenatal anti-D given at 28 and 34 weeks, 625 IU each dose
Postpartum anti-D within 72 hours if baby is rhesus D positive
Additional doses for sensitising events (bleeding, ECV, trauma, CVS, amniocentesis)
Antibody screen at 28 weeks before prophylaxis to detect prior sensitisation
Kleihauer test after large fetomaternal haemorrhage to guide additional dosing
explanation.pearls: An additional 625 IU dose covers up to 6 mL fetomaternal haemorrhage; a Kleihauer test above this threshold prompts dose escalation calculated per millilitre.

stem: You are the obstetric registrar called to assess a 29-year-old G1P0 non-Aboriginal woman at 32 weeks gestation referred from a regional clinic with new severe hypertension, headache and visual disturbance. She has had no fits. Her urine is positive for protein 3+ and her platelets are mildly reduced. The fetal heart trace is normal and there is no antepartum haemorrhage. She has had a single dose of betamethasone at the referring service one hour ago.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 168/118 repeated 165/116, HR 96, RR 18, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Headache, photophobia, brisk reflexes with two beats of clonus, no abdominal tenderness, fetal heart rate 140 with normal variability
data_table.Investigations: Urine protein 3+, platelets 118 x10^9/L, ALT 64 U/L, creatinine 78 micromol/L, normal coagulation, fundi normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Oral labetalol 200 mg, magnesium sulfate loading dose 4 g intravenous and arrange transfer to tertiary obstetric unit with maternal and fetal monitoring
options.rationale: Oral antihypertensive is slow to act in severe hypertension with end-organ symptoms; intravenous antihypertensive is required for rapid control alongside magnesium and transfer. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Intravenous hydralazine 20 mg bolus, no magnesium until any seizure occurs, and immediate emergency caesarean section at the referring regional service today
options.rationale: Magnesium is given prophylactically once severe pre-eclampsia is diagnosed, before any seizure; immediate delivery at a regional centre without neonatal capability for 32 weeks is unsafe when transfer is possible. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Sublingual nifedipine 10 mg every 15 minutes until BP under 140/90 and admit for observation without magnesium sulfate or steroids
options.rationale: Sublingual nifedipine causes precipitous drops in blood pressure with fetal compromise; oral immediate-release nifedipine is acceptable but never sublingual, and magnesium is indicated. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Intravenous labetalol 20 mg bolus titrated, magnesium sulfate 4 g loading then 1 g per hour, second betamethasone dose at 24 hours and transfer to tertiary unit
options.rationale: Severe pre-eclampsia at 32 weeks needs rapid intravenous BP control, magnesium for seizure prophylaxis, completion of corticosteroid course for fetal lung maturity and transfer to a tertiary unit with neonatal intensive care. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Intravenous frusemide 20 mg, magnesium sulfate infusion and methyldopa 250 mg three times daily without immediate intravenous antihypertensive cover
options.rationale: Diuretics are not indicated unless pulmonary oedema is present and methyldopa is too slow for severe acute hypertension; intravenous labetalol or hydralazine is required. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Severe pre-eclampsia is diagnosed by BP at or above 160/110 with proteinuria or with any organ involvement. Immediate priorities are rapid BP control (intravenous labetalol or hydralazine, or oral immediate-release nifedipine), magnesium sulfate seizure prophylaxis, antenatal corticosteroid course completion if under 35 weeks, and transfer to a tertiary obstetric unit. Delivery is the only definitive treatment but timing balances maternal stability against fetal maturity; expectant management may continue at 32 weeks once stable.
key_points: Target BP under 160/110 within an hour using intravenous labetalol, hydralazine or oral nifedipine
Magnesium sulfate 4 g loading then 1 g per hour for seizure prophylaxis once severe
Antenatal corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity if under 35 weeks
Sublingual nifedipine is contraindicated because of precipitous drops
Transfer to a tertiary unit with NICU support before delivery if maternal and fetal stable
explanation.pearls: Loss of patellar reflexes is the earliest sign of magnesium toxicity, occurring at a serum level of 5 to 6 mmol/L before respiratory depression at 7.5 mmol/L.

stem: You are the obstetric registrar called urgently to the birthing suite for a 30-year-old G3P2 non-Aboriginal woman at 38 weeks gestation in spontaneous labour. The midwife performed artificial rupture of membranes for slow progress and immediately felt a pulsating loop of cord at the cervix. The cervix is 6 cm dilated and the presenting part is high. The cardiotocograph now shows prolonged bradycardia at 70 beats per minute. There is no antepartum haemorrhage.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 122/78, HR 96, RR 18, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Cervix 6 cm dilated, vertex above ischial spines, palpable pulsating cord through cervix
data_table.Investigations: CTG fetal bradycardia 70 bpm, reduced variability

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate action?

options.text: Augment with oxytocin to speed second stage and prepare for instrumental vaginal delivery in the birthing suite under regional anaesthesia
options.rationale: Oxytocin worsens cord compression by increasing contractions; the cervix is not fully dilated so vaginal delivery is not possible and emergency caesarean is required. (Source: RANZCOG Cord Prolapse Statement)

options.text: Position in left lateral, replace the cord into the vagina warmly and arrange transfer to theatre for category 2 caesarean within 60 minutes of decision
options.rationale: Cord prolapse with abnormal fetal heart trace is a category 1 caesarean decision because of immediate fetal compromise; 60 minutes is too slow. (Source: RANZCOG Cord Prolapse Statement)

options.text: Knee-chest or steep Trendelenburg position, elevate the presenting part manually off the cord, prepare for category 1 caesarean and call paediatrics
options.rationale: Elevating the presenting part and adopting knee-chest position relieves cord compression while preparing for immediate category 1 caesarean delivery within 30 minutes of decision. (Source: RANZCOG Cord Prolapse Statement)

options.text: Insert a urinary catheter and instill 500 mL warm saline into the bladder to elevate the presenting part, then proceed to elective caesarean later today
options.rationale: Bladder filling is a useful adjunct only when there is transport delay; even then, definitive management is immediate category 1 caesarean rather than elective deferral. (Source: RANZCOG Cord Prolapse Statement)

options.text: Reassess CTG after 10 minutes in left lateral and proceed to caesarean only if bradycardia persists, otherwise continue labour with continuous monitoring
options.rationale: Cord prolapse with bradycardia is an immediate fetal emergency; waiting 10 minutes loses critical time and risks hypoxic-ischaemic injury. (Source: RANZCOG Cord Prolapse Statement)

explanation.summary: Umbilical cord prolapse is an obstetric emergency. Immediate management aims to relieve cord compression while organising delivery: manual elevation of the presenting part through the vagina, knee-chest or steep Trendelenburg position, tocolysis if uterine activity is contributing, and bladder filling only if transfer to theatre is delayed. Delivery is by category 1 caesarean within 30 minutes of decision unless the cervix is fully dilated and instrumental delivery is rapidly achievable. Neonatal resuscitation team must attend.
key_points: Manual elevation of the presenting part is the first physical action
Knee-chest or steep Trendelenburg position relieves cord compression
Category 1 caesarean within 30 minutes of decision if cervix not fully dilated
Bladder filling is an adjunct only when delivery is delayed
Tocolysis with terbutaline 250 micrograms subcutaneous reduces contractions during transfer
explanation.pearls: Subcutaneous terbutaline 250 micrograms is the recommended tocolytic during transfer for cord prolapse, providing 15 to 30 minutes of uterine relaxation.
